currency.name: Botswana Pula
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 100 BWP (the word "pula" means "rain", a precious thing here)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Botswana on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, in the south. Botswana is landlocked, surrounded by Namibia, South Africa, Zambia and Zimbabwe 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Botswana?
mapQuestions.answer: Gaborone, in the south-east. About 250,000 people, near the South African border. Pronounced "hah-bo-ROH-neh".

mapQuestions.question: What language does Botswana speak?
mapQuestions.answer: English is the official language, and Setswana is the national language spoken by almost everyone. The people are called "Batswana" (one person is a "Motswana").

mapQuestions.question: How wild is Botswana?
mapQuestions.answer: Nearly 40% of the country is protected as national parks, game reserves and wildlife management areas. Some of the wildest country in southern Africa.

mapQuestions.question: What does "pula" mean?
mapQuestions.answer: In Setswana, "pula" means "rain". It is the country's national motto AND the name of its currency. In dry Botswana, rain is the most precious thing of all.

foods.name: Bogobe
foods.description: A soft sorghum or maize porridge, the Setswana staple. Eaten with stews or sour milk.

foods.name: Seswaa
foods.description: Slow-pounded shredded beef cooked simply with salt and a bone, served on top of bogobe. The Setswana national dish, made for weddings and big celebrations.

foods.name: Mopane worms
foods.description: Dried mopane caterpillars, pan-fried with onion and tomato. A nutritious and beloved Botswana snack. (Yes, really. Try them.)

foods.name: Vetkoek + mince
foods.description: A pillowy deep-fried dough ball stuffed with seasoned minced beef. The Botswana road-trip standard.

foods.name: Braai chicken
foods.description: Chicken portions grilled over open wood coals at a "braai" (BBQ), basted with a sticky South African marinade.

foods.name: Morogo
foods.description: Wild leafy greens cooked with onion and tomato. Foraged from the bush. The traditional vegetable side dish.

scavengerItems.label: A huge elephant herd in Chobe National Park (more elephants than anywhere on Earth)
Hippopotamus pods in the Chobe River
A "mokoro" dugout canoe gliding through the Okavango Delta reeds
Migrating zebras in the Makgadikgadi Pans (one of the longest zebra migrations on Earth)
A lion pride hunting at dawn in the Kalahari
A giraffe drinking with legs splayed (a moment of vulnerability)
African buffalo, often in herds of hundreds in Chobe
A simple bush-camp tent under a thorn tree
A bateleur eagle (Africa's acrobat-eagle, tail-less, painted red and black)
Wild dogs (painted wolves) hunting (Botswana has Africa's biggest population)
The Milky Way blazing across the sky in the Kalahari

geoMatches.description: The quiet capital, in the south-east. Modern, sleepy, surrounded by hills. Most travellers fly through, not into.
The world's biggest inland delta: a 15,000 sq km maze of reed channels and palm islands where the Okavango River SPREADS OUT into the Kalahari and never reaches the sea. UNESCO World Heritage.
In the north, famous for having the densest concentration of African elephants on Earth (about 130,000). Easy game-driving on the Chobe River.
A semi-desert covering most of Botswana. Not Sahara-style dunes, but huge red sandy plains with thorn trees and isolated waterholes.
A vast salt pan landscape, the dried-out bed of an ancient super-lake. In the wet season, zebras migrate through. In the dry, it is a moonscape.
A small set of quartzite hills sticking out of the Kalahari, with over 4,500 ancient San rock paintings. UNESCO World Heritage, sometimes called the "Louvre of the Desert".

senses.smell: Dust on a Kalahari road, woodsmoke from a bush camp, fresh reeds in the Okavango Delta, the metallic smell of rain on dry earth ("petrichor"), wild sage…
senses.hear: "Dumela!" greetings everywhere, the slap of mokoro paddles on water, lions roaring at night, elephants trumpeting at a waterhole, the silence of the Kalahari…
senses.taste: Tender pounded seswaa beef, smoky braai chicken, sour fermented milk, crispy mopane worms, fresh papaya at breakfast…
senses.feel: Red Kalahari sand sticking to your shoes, the cool water of the Okavango against your hand from a mokoro, the chill of a desert dawn, the warm bark of an ancient baobab…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How is the Okavango a delta in the middle of the desert?
stories.body: Most river deltas form where a river meets the sea. The Okavango is different: it flows out of the highlands of Angola, runs about 1,000 km south-east into the Kalahari Desert, and then SPREADS OUT into a giant 15,000 square km wetland, never reaching the ocean. The water just slowly evaporates, soaks away, or is drunk by the wildlife. The delta is a maze of clear water channels and palm-tree islands, and in flood season (June to August) it fills with migrating elephants, hippos, lions, and 400+ bird species. Most travellers explore the delta by "mokoro" (a dugout canoe poled by a guide standing at the back), gliding through papyrus reeds for hours at a time.

stories.question: How many elephants live in Botswana?
stories.body: About 130,000 African elephants live in Botswana, which is roughly a THIRD of all the wild African elephants left on Earth. Most of them live in the Chobe-Okavango ecosystem in the north. Family herds of 50 or more cross the Chobe River every evening to drink, the calves swimming in the protected middle of the herd. Bull elephants sometimes mock-charge boats (don't worry, the guide always knows the difference). Botswana's elephant population has GROWN over the last 30 years thanks to strict anti-poaching laws and large protected areas, while elephants in many other African countries have declined.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Botswana protects elephants so well that herds keep growing. What can the rest of the world learn from that?

stories.question: Who are the San / Bushmen?
stories.body: The San (also called Bushmen, or sometimes "Basarwa" in Botswana) are descended from the oldest continuous human population on Earth. Genetic studies suggest the San lineage split from the rest of humanity over 100,000 years ago. They lived as hunter-gatherers across southern Africa for tens of thousands of years, with deep knowledge of every plant, animal track and water hole. Their click-consonant languages are some of the most complex on Earth. Today most San live in small communities in Botswana and Namibia, balancing traditional knowledge with modern life. You can sometimes walk with San guides in the Kalahari and learn how they read the desert.

stories.question: Why is Botswana such a quiet success story?
stories.body: When Botswana became independent from Britain in 1966, it was one of the poorest countries in the world: no infrastructure, very little education, no university, almost no paved roads. Then in 1967, MASSIVE diamond deposits were discovered. Botswana made an unusual choice: it set up a partnership (50/50) with the diamond company De Beers, and used the diamond money to slowly build schools, hospitals, roads and government institutions instead of letting it all flow to a few rich people. Today Botswana is one of Africa's most stable democracies, with one of the highest per-person incomes on the continent. It has held free, peaceful elections every five years since 1966. It is sometimes called "the African miracle".

stories.question: What's the "Louvre of the Desert"?
stories.body: Sticking out of the Kalahari sand near the Namibian border are the Tsodilo Hills, a small group of quartzite peaks no higher than 400 metres. The hills are covered in over 4,500 rock paintings made by San artists over the last 100,000+ years, with the oldest dating back tens of thousands of years. The paintings show eland (the biggest African antelope), giraffes, fish, dancing figures and complex geometric patterns. UNESCO put Tsodilo on the World Heritage list in 2001. The hills are sacred to the local San and Hambukushu peoples, and visiting them is meant to be done slowly and respectfully.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Botswana so far?
Have you tried "dumela" or "ke a leboga" yet?
Delta, desert, salt pan, parks, which has felt the most "Botswana"?
Did you ride in a mokoro? What was the silence like?
If we had to choose ONE Botswana animal to remember, which would it be?
Botswana used its diamond wealth carefully and built a stable country. What does that show about how countries can shape their future?

wordSearch.hint: Botswanan foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: Chobe National Park has the highest density of African elephants on Earth (around 130,000 in the country).

animals.name: Hippopotamus
animals.description: Big pods in the Okavango Delta channels, often grunting near mokoro routes.

animals.name: Lion
animals.description: Across the delta and Kalahari. The Okavango lions have learned to swim and hunt buffalo through the floods.

animals.name: African buffalo
animals.description: Big dark herds in Chobe and the Okavango. Heavy, dangerous, always in groups.

animals.name: Plains zebra
animals.description: Botswana hosts one of Africa's longest zebra migrations, between the Okavango and the Makgadikgadi pans.

animals.name: African wild dog
animals.description: Botswana has Africa's biggest wild-dog population. Painted with brown, black, white and yellow patches. Endangered.

animals.name: Leopard
animals.description: In the riverine forests of the Okavango, often draped on the branch of a sausage-tree at sunset.
